sections.heading: RT
sections.content: Radiation therapy (RT) is not a standard primary treatment for epithelial ovarian cancer due to low efficacy and high toxicity. It may be used for palliation of symptoms or for oligometastatic disease in recurrent settings. Principles: Whole abdominal RT is rarely used at NCCN Member Institutions. Localized RT can be considered to palliate symptoms (e.g., bone metastases, brain metastases) or for oligometastatic disease. RT is not part of primary adjuvant treatment for epithelial ovarian cancer. Dose frameworks: Palliative RT for bone metastases: 30 Gy in 10 fractions or 8 Gy in 1 fraction (3 Gy or 8 Gy per fraction, 10 or 1 fractions, Daily or single fraction) - Indication: Symptomatic bone metastases. Palliative RT for brain metastases: 30 Gy in 10 fractions or 20 Gy in 5 fractions (3 Gy or 4 Gy per fraction, 10 or 5 fractions, Daily) - Indication: Symptomatic brain metastases. Approaches: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT): dose fractionation varies by indication; typically palliative dosing. Concurrent chemotherapy not standard in ovarian cancer. Indication: Palliation of symptoms from metastases. Key trial: N/A. Toxicities: Fatigue, nausea, diarrhea, myelosuppression depending on field.

timeline.title: Palliative RT for bone metastases
timeline.body: 30 Gy in 10 fractions or 8 Gy in 1 fraction. Daily or single fraction. Indication: Symptomatic bone metastases.

timeline.title: Palliative RT for brain metastases
timeline.body: 30 Gy in 10 fractions or 20 Gy in 5 fractions. Daily. Indication: Symptomatic brain metastases.
